flashcards.front: What are Domain Constraints in a relational database, and how do they differ from key constraints?
flashcards.back: Domain constraints specify the valid values for an attribute, enforced via data types, NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK clauses, and default values. Key constraints (primary key, candidate key) ensure entity integrity by uniquely identifying tuples, while domain constraints ensure attribute-level validity. Example: age BETWEEN 0 AND 150 is a domain constraint; roll_no PRIMARY KEY is a key constraint.

flashcards.front: Define referential integrity and explain the four referential actions (policies) when a referenced tuple is deleted or updated.
flashcards.back: Referential integrity ensures foreign key values in a relation match primary key values in the referenced relation. The four referential actions are: CASCADE (propagate delete/update to matching tuples), SET NULL (set foreign key to NULL), SET DEFAULT (set foreign key to default value), and RESTRICT (reject delete/update if matching tuples exist). Example: DELETE CASCADE on orders when customer is deleted removes all their orders.

flashcards.front: Distinguish between total (mandatory) and partial (optional) participation constraints in ER modeling with examples.
flashcards.back: Total participation (mandatory) means every entity in the entity set MUST participate in at least one relationship (shown as double line in ER diagram). Partial participation (optional) means entities may or may not participate (single line). Example: Every STUDENT must enroll in at least one COURSE (total participation), but a COURSE may have no students enrolled (partial participation). In relational schema, total participation is enforced via NOT NULL foreign key.

flashcards.front: Explain cardinality ratios (1:1, 1:N, M:N) in relationships with practical examples and how they are implemented in relational schema.
flashcards.back: Cardinality specifies maximum number of entities in one entity set that can associate with entities in another set. 1:1 (one-to-one) - e.g., one MANAGER per DEPARTMENT; implement with shared primary key or foreign key. 1:N (one-to-many) - e.g., one DEPARTMENT has many EMPLOYEES; implement foreign key in the 'many' side. M:N (many-to-many) - e.g., STUDENT enrolls in many COURSEs and each COURSE has many STUDENTs; implement via separate relationship table with composite primary key.

flashcards.front: What is a weak entity set in ER modeling? How does it differ from a strong entity set, and how are its relationships represented?
flashcards.back: A weak entity set lacks sufficient attributes to form a primary key (depends on a strong entity for identification), represented by double rectangle. A strong entity has a primary key and exists independently. Weak entities are identified by combining their partial key with the primary key of the owner entity via a identifying relationship (double diamond). Example: DEPENDENT is weak (identified by name + emp_id from EMPLOYEE); EMPLOYEE is strong with emp_id as primary key.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a candidate key, primary key, alternate key, and composite key with examples?
flashcards.back: Candidate key: minimal superkey that uniquely identifies tuples (e.g., emp_id, aadhaar_no in EMPLOYEE). Primary key: selected candidate key used for identification (emp_id). Alternate key: remaining candidate keys not chosen as primary (aadhaar_no). Composite key: key with multiple attributes (order_id + product_id identifies line_item). All candidate keys must be NOT NULL and UNIQUE; only one becomes primary key per relation.

flashcards.front: Explain the referential integrity violation scenarios when updating a primary key value in the parent relation and how to handle them.
flashcards.back: Updating a primary key in parent relation can violate referential integrity if child rows reference the old value. Solutions: ON UPDATE clause options (CASCADE propagates new PK to all foreign keys; SET NULL sets FK to NULL; SET DEFAULT sets to default; RESTRICT rejects update). Best practice: avoid updating PKs in production systems; use surrogate keys (auto-increment IDs) to prevent referential integrity issues. Example: Changing dept_id from D001 to D100 requires CASCADE to update all emp.dept_id references.

flashcards.front: How would you enforce the following business rule in a university database: 'Each student must enroll in exactly 3 courses per semester, and a course can have 10-100 students'
flashcards.back: This requires multiple constraint layers: (1) Domain constraint on enrollment count: CHECK (semester_credits = 3) - enforced via application logic or trigger. (2) Participation constraint: total participation of STUDENT in ENROLL relationship. (3) Cardinality: M:N relationship between STUDENT and COURSE. (4) Additional CHECK constraints: course_capacity BETWEEN 10 AND 100. (5) To enforce exactly 3 courses: use a trigger that counts enrollments per student per semester and raises exception if not equal to 3. Relational implementation: ENROLLMENT(student_id, course_id, semester, grade) with composite PK and additional constraints.
